attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old woman presents to the hospital antenatal clinic at 25 weeks gestation following amniocentesis results. She is gravida 2 para 1 with one previous uncomplicated pregnancy. She has no significant medical history and takes only pregnancy vitamins. The amniocentesis was performed following concerning findings on morphology ultrasound, including cardiac defects and shortened long bones. The results confirm trisomy 21. After extensive counselling and consideration, she has decided to terminate the pregnancy.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. The uterine size is consistent with dates. An ultrasound confirms a viable singleton pregnancy with fetal movements.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Induction of labour with prostaglandins alone
Immediate medical termination with mifepristone and misoprostol
Caesarean delivery followed by palliative care
attributes.explanation:
This case presents an ethically and emotionally challenging scenario of late termination of pregnancy following diagnosis of a major chromosomal abnormality. The gestational age of 25 weeks is beyond the threshold where signs of life are possible at delivery, necessitating specific management considerations.

Feticide followed by medical termination is the appropriate management in this situation. Feticide (usually performed by ultrasound-guided intracardiac potassium chloride injection) ensures there are no signs of life at delivery, which is important both legally and for the psychological wellbeing of the parents and healthcare staff. Following feticide, medical termination with mifepristone (for cervical preparation) followed by misoprostol (for uterine contractions) is the standard protocol.

Immediate medical termination with mifepristone and misoprostol alone would be inappropriate at this gestation as it could result in a live birth with subsequent death, which would be both legally and ethically problematic.

Surgical termination is not feasible at this advanced gestation due to the size of the fetus and the increased risk of complications.

Induction of labour with prostaglandins alone would be less effective than the combined medical approach with mifepristone and misoprostol, and again would not address the issue of potential signs of life at delivery.

Caesarean delivery would expose the mother to unnecessary surgical risks and would not be appropriate in this situation where termination has been requested.
learningPoints: Late termination of pregnancy (>22 weeks) requires feticide prior to induction of labour to ensure no signs of life at delivery.
Feticide is performed by intracardiac potassium chloride injection under ultrasound guidance.
Medical termination with mifepristone and misoprostol is the preferred method for second trimester termination of pregnancy.
references: Queensland Clinical Guidelines. Termination of pregnancy [Internet]. Brisbane: Queensland Health; 2024 Sep. Report No.: MN24.21-V10-R29. [cited 2024 Nov 29]. Available from: https://www.health.qld.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0029/735293/g-top.pdf

Rosser S, Sekar R, Laporte J, Duncombe GJ, Bendall A, Lehner C, et al. Late termination of pregnancy at a major Queensland tertiary hospital, 2010–2020. Med J Aust. 2022;217(8):377-82. Available from: https://www.mja.com.au/system/files/issues/217_08/mja251697.pdf
Loughnan M. Feticide and late termination of pregnancy. O&G Magazine. 2020;20(2):20-3. Available from: https://www.ogmagazine.org.au/20/2-20/feticide-and-late-termination-of-pregnancy/
